"And he heard the words of Laban's sons, saying, Jacob hath taken away all that was our father's; and of that which was our father's hath he gotten all this glory." The witnesses note the envy of Laban's sons at Jacob's prosperity. Gill notes Jacob heard the words of Laban's sons, either with his own ears, overhearing their discourse in their tents or in the field, or from the report of others, his wives or friends. The witnesses note Jacob hearing Laban's sons complain that he had taken away all that was their father's and gotten his wealth; the envious murmuring that warned Jacob his welcome was worn out — that Jacob, now greatly increased, "heard the words of Laban's sons" murmuring against him: "Jacob hath taken away all that was our father's; and of that which was our father's hath he gotten all this glory"; the witnesses note the spirit of the complaint — it is the voice of envy and covetousness, the sons of Laban grudging Jacob's God-given prosperity and falsely charging him with having stripped their father, when in truth his wealth was the just reward of his labour and God's blessing; the witnesses note that this murmuring was the first sign to Jacob that his time in Padan-aram was ending, the household turning against him; so that the envy of Laban's sons becomes the providential occasion warning Jacob to look homeward; of that which was our father's hath he gotten all this glory. The witnesses note Jacob hath taken away all that was our father's. The witnesses note of that which was our father's hath he gotten all this glory.

The witnesses note Laban's charge that Jacob not afflict his daughters nor take other wives, with God as witness. Adam Clarke notes "no man is with us" — though all were present at the sacrifice, in the agreement about the daughters it was a matter between Jacob and God alone, no human witness being able to testify. The witnesses note Laban charging Jacob not to afflict his daughters nor take other wives, since God is witness between them; the fatherly stipulation guarding the daughters, sealed by the witness of God — that Laban's chief concern in the covenant is for his daughters: "If thou shalt afflict my daughters, or if thou shalt take other wives beside my daughters, no man is with us; see, God is witness betwixt me and thee"; the witnesses note the two stipulations — that Jacob not "afflict" Rachel and Leah (by ill-usage, cruelty, or neglect) and that he not "take other wives beside" them (to their grief and dishonour); the witnesses note the appeal to the divine witness — "no man is with us" to see how Jacob treats his wives in the privacy of his home, but "God is witness," the all-seeing One who beholds what no man can; the witnesses note that this is the one point in Laban that shows a father's true care, guarding his daughters' welfare by binding Jacob before God; so that the covenant binds Jacob to faithful, kind treatment of his wives under the eye of God; God is witness betwixt me and thee. The witnesses note If thou shalt afflict my daughters, or if thou shalt take other wives beside my daughters. The witnesses note no man is with us; see, God is witness betwixt me and thee.

The witnesses note Laban's fatherly charge that Jacob not afflict his daughters nor take other wives — sealed by the appeal to God as witness, who sees in the home's privacy what no man can.

So the witnesses make the verse the stipulation guarding the daughters under God's witness. The lesson the expositors draw is that Laban bound Jacob not to afflict or supplant his daughters, appealing to God who sees what no man can. The witnesses note the afflict my daughters: "If thou shalt afflict my daughters" — by ill-usage or neglect; the afflict my daughters; the ill-usage; the cruelty; the neglect; the grief. The expositors observe the other wives: "or if thou shalt take other wives" — to their grief and dishonour; the other wives; the to their grief; the dishonour; the supplant; the beside them. 51:
"And Laban said to Jacob, Behold this heap, and behold this pillar, which I have cast betwixt me and thee;" The witnesses note Laban pointing to the heap and pillar as the tokens of the covenant. Adam Clarke notes that this pillar was set up by Jacob (v.45), not cast by Laban; Laban speaks of it as the common monument of the agreement. Gill notes Laban continued speaking to Jacob, pointing to the heap and pillar. The witnesses note Laban bidding Jacob behold the heap and the pillar set between them; the formal pointing to the twin monuments that sealed the covenant — that Laban, drawing the covenant to its solemn close, points to the visible tokens: "Behold this heap, and behold this pillar, which I have cast betwixt me and thee"; the witnesses note the two monuments named — "this heap" (the gathered stones, Galeed) and "this pillar" (the single standing stone Jacob had set up, v.45), both set "betwixt me and thee" as the standing witnesses of the agreement; the witnesses note Clarke's careful observation that the pillar was in fact set up by Jacob, not "cast" by Laban, so that Laban speaks loosely, claiming a share in the common monument; the witnesses note that the heap and pillar together form the double, enduring witness to which Laban now points as he states the terms of the covenant; so that Laban directs Jacob's eye to the standing tokens that would witness the bond between them; Behold this heap, and behold this pillar. The witnesses note Behold this heap, and behold this pillar. The witnesses note which I have cast betwixt me and thee.

The witnesses note Laban pointing to the heap and pillar as the twin witnesses of the covenant — though, as Clarke notes, the pillar was in fact set up by Jacob, Laban claiming a share in the common monument.

So the witnesses make the verse Laban pointing to the twin monuments of the covenant. The lesson the expositors draw is that Laban directed Jacob's eye to the heap and pillar as the standing witnesses of their agreement. The witnesses note the behold this heap: "Behold this heap" — the gathered stones, Galeed; the behold this heap; the the stones; the Galeed; the the monument; the witness. The expositors observe the behold this pillar: "and behold this pillar" — the single standing stone; the behold this pillar; the the pillar; the standing stone; the Jacob set up; the monument. The witnesses press the betwixt me and thee: "which I have cast betwixt me and thee" — set between them; the betwixt me and thee; the between them; the set; the the witnesses; the the agreement. The expositors note the double witness: the heap and pillar a double witness; the double witness; the the heap; the the pillar; the enduring; the witness. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Behold this heap and behold this pillar, the which I have cast betwixt me and thee, and the twin monuments. The verse thus records Laban pointing to the twin monuments — teaching that Laban directed Jacob's eye to the heap and pillar as the standing witnesses of their agreement.
52:
"This heap be witness, and this pillar be witness, that I will not pass over this heap to thee, and that thou shalt not pass over this heap and this pillar unto me, for harm." The witnesses note the heap and pillar made witness to a mutual boundary of non-aggression. Gill notes Laban, agreeably to the name given the heap, makes it a witness that neither party would pass over it to harm the other. The witnesses note Laban declaring the heap and pillar witness that neither he nor Jacob would pass over to the other for harm; the boundary-covenant of mutual non-aggression, sealed by the monuments — that Laban states the heart of the agreement: "This heap be witness, and this pillar be witness, that I will not pass over this heap to thee, and that thou shalt not pass over this heap and this pillar unto me, for harm"; the witnesses note the substance of the covenant — the heap and pillar are made the boundary-mark and witness of a mutual pledge of non-aggression: that Laban "will not pass over" to Jacob's side, nor Jacob over to Laban's, "for harm," that is, with hostile intent; the witnesses note the equal and reciprocal terms — each binds himself not to cross the boundary against the other, so that the monument marks a frontier of peace between the two and their descendants; the witnesses note that the covenant, born of distrust, yet secures peace by setting a clear and witnessed limit; so that the heap and pillar stand as the witnessed boundary of peace between Jacob and Laban; that thou shalt not pass over this heap and this pillar unto me, for harm. The witnesses note This heap be witness, and this pillar be witness. The witnesses note that I will not pass over this heap to thee... for harm.

The witnesses note the heap and pillar made the witnessed boundary of mutual non-aggression — neither party to pass over for harm; a frontier of peace, born of distrust yet securing peace by a clear limit.

So the witnesses make the verse the boundary-covenant of non-aggression. The lesson the expositors draw is that the heap and pillar witness a mutual pledge that neither would cross the boundary against the other for harm. 53:
"The God of Abraham, and the God of Nahor, the God of their father, judge betwixt us. And Jacob sware by the fear of his father Isaac." The witnesses note the oath invoking God as judge, and Jacob swearing by the fear of Isaac. Gill notes the father of Abraham and Nahor was Terah, whose gods, while idolaters, are not the true God meant; Jacob does not swear by these, but by the fear of his father Isaac, the true God. The witnesses note Laban invoking the God of Abraham and of Nahor to judge between them, while Jacob swore by the fear of his father Isaac; the oath that seals the covenant, with Jacob carefully swearing by the true God alone — that the covenant is sealed with a solemn oath, but the two men do not name God alike: "The God of Abraham, and the God of Nahor, the God of their father, judge betwixt us. And Jacob sware by the fear of his father Isaac"; the witnesses note the careful distinction — Laban invokes "the God of Abraham, and the God of Nahor, the God of their father" (Terah), lumping together the true God and the idols of his idolatrous house; but Jacob, refusing to confound the true God with Laban's gods, "sware by the fear of his father Isaac" alone (the true and living God whom Isaac reverenced); the witnesses note the debate over Laban's phrase — whether he meant the one true God of the fathers, or, in his confusion, the gods of Nahor's idolatry; the witnesses note Jacob's exact piety, swearing only by the true God; so that the covenant is sworn, Jacob distinguishing the true God from Laban's idols; And Jacob sware by the fear of his father Isaac. The witnesses note The God of Abraham, and the God of Nahor, the God of their father, judge betwixt us. The witnesses note And Jacob sware by the fear of his father Isaac.

The witnesses note Laban invoking "the God of Abraham and of Nahor," lumping true God and idols, while Jacob carefully swore by "the fear of his father Isaac" alone — refusing to confound the true God with Laban's gods.

So the witnesses make the verse the oath sealing the covenant, with Jacob swearing by the true God alone. The lesson the expositors draw is that Laban invoked the God of the fathers loosely, but Jacob swore only by the fear of Isaac, the true God. The witnesses note the God of Abraham and Nahor: "The God of Abraham, and the God of Nahor" — Gill notes Terah's house mixed idols; the God of Abraham and Nahor; the Terah; the idols; the the fathers; the mixed. 54:
"Then Jacob offered sacrifice upon the mount, and called his brethren to eat bread: and they did eat bread, and tarried all night in the mount." The witnesses note Jacob offering sacrifice and the covenant feast on the mount. Gill notes Jacob offered sacrifice upon mount Gilead — not in a religious way in which he could join Laban, but he slew cattle for a feast, as was usual at the making of covenants. The witnesses note Jacob offering sacrifice on the mount and calling his brethren to eat bread, and they ate and lodged all night; the sacrificial covenant-feast that ratified the peace, kept among Jacob's own — that Jacob seals the covenant with a sacrifice and a feast: "Jacob offered sacrifice upon the mount, and called his brethren to eat bread: and they did eat bread, and tarried all night in the mount"; the witnesses note the acts — Jacob "offered sacrifice" (whether a true offering to God, or, as Gill suggests, the slaying of cattle for the covenant-feast, as was customary at such agreements) and "called his brethren to eat bread," the shared covenant-meal that ratified the peace; the witnesses note the careful detail that this was kept among Jacob's "brethren" (his own kinsmen and company), and that all "tarried all night in the mount," lodging together before parting; the witnesses note that the feast, with its sacrifice, set a solemn and sacred seal on the covenant of peace; so that the covenant is ratified with sacrifice and the breaking of bread, and the company rests for the night; they did eat bread, and tarried all night in the mount. The witnesses note Jacob offered sacrifice upon the mount, and called his brethren to eat bread. The witnesses note and they did eat bread, and tarried all night in the mount.

The witnesses note Jacob sealing the covenant with sacrifice and a shared feast on the mount — the covenant-meal ratifying the peace, the company lodging together before parting; a solemn, sacred seal.

So the witnesses make the verse the sacrificial covenant-feast ratifying the peace. The lesson the expositors draw is that Jacob sealed the covenant with sacrifice and a shared meal, the company lodging together before parting. 55:
"And early in the morning Laban rose up, and kissed his sons and his daughters, and blessed them: and Laban departed, and returned unto his place." The witnesses note Laban's affectionate farewell and departure home. Gill notes early in the morning Laban rose to set out on his journey home, and kissed his sons (his grandsons) and his daughters, Rachel and Leah, with Dinah, as was the custom of relations. The witnesses note Laban rising early, kissing and blessing his sons and daughters, then departing and returning to his place; the peaceful parting at last, Laban taking the farewell he had complained of being denied — that the long conflict ends in a quiet and kindly parting: "early in the morning Laban rose up, and kissed his sons and his daughters, and blessed them: and Laban departed, and returned unto his place"; the witnesses note the change from the hostile pursuer to the affectionate kinsman — Laban now takes the very leave-taking he had earlier complained of being denied (v.28), kissing "his sons" (his grandsons) and "his daughters" (Rachel and Leah), and "blessed them" with a parting blessing; the witnesses note that, the covenant made and the feast shared, Laban "departed, and returned unto his place," going home in peace; the witnesses note the providence that brought the dangerous meeting to so peaceful an end — the pursuer who came to harm Jacob now blesses and leaves him; so that the whole conflict with Laban closes in peace, and Jacob is free to continue homeward; Laban departed, and returned unto his place. The witnesses note Laban rose up, and kissed his sons and his daughters, and blessed them. The witnesses note and Laban departed, and returned unto his place.

The witnesses note Laban's peaceful farewell at last — kissing and blessing his grandsons and daughters, then departing home; the hostile pursuer who came to harm Jacob now blesses and leaves him in peace.

So the witnesses make the verse the peaceful parting of Laban from Jacob.
